entries.title: Two-maintainer concentration + 4.7% formal review on a privileged remote-execution tool
entries.action_hint: Pin to a reviewed release tag; verify publisher account security before major upgrades.
entries.threat_model_context: Kamal is a privileged deployment orchestrator — it runs SSH + docker commands as the operator against every target server. A supply-chain compromise (credential leak on djmb or dhh, a malicious gem push) reaches every kamal user's production infrastructure. The review gate that should gate that risk shows 4.7% formal review and 51% self-merge across the last 300 PRs, with no CODEOWNERS to force pathwise scrutiny.
entries.what_this_means: Most changes on main have landed without independent human review. Downstream operators install whatever the current release tag points at, which was produced by a pipeline without a second-human gate.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of malicious behavior by either maintainer. This is a structural observation about the review model on a tool whose blast radius — root-level command execution on deployment targets — deserves a stronger gate than a research gem would need.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add CODEOWNERS naming 2 reviewers; upgrade the ruleset to require approving reviews (not just advisory Copilot review) before merge to main. Consumer-side: pin to a specific reviewed release tag and read the CHANGELOG before upgrades; enable RubyGems MFA enforcement on the org account.

entries.title: No SECURITY.md on a privileged deployment tool
entries.action_hint: Any vulnerability you find must be reported via the public issue tracker — no documented private channel.
entries.threat_model_context: Kamal runs with SSH + docker privileges against every deployment target its users have configured. A pre-patched vulnerability disclosed to the maintainers privately could be coordinated with a release; a vulnerability posted to the public issue tracker is live before any fix. No SECURITY.md = no documented private channel.
entries.what_this_means: Vulnerability reporters with a pre-patched finding default to the public issue tracker because there's no documented alternative. For a tool with kamal's blast radius, this is a process gap that widens the disclosure-to-fix window.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: GitHub's 'Report a vulnerability' button (private-advisory channel) may still work — but its use is implicit rather than documented. Not a code defect; a process defect.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add a 1-page SECURITY.md naming the 'Report a vulnerability' button under the Security tab, expected response window, and GPG fingerprints of the maintainer accounts.

entries.title: Dependabot config watches only github-actions — RubyGems deps unwatched
entries.action_hint: Add `bundler` to dependabot.yml ecosystems; consumer-side run `bundle audit` locally.
entries.threat_model_context: Kamal declares 10 runtime Ruby dependencies (including sshkit + net-ssh, which carry Kamal's remote-execution capability). Dependabot.yml tracks only the `github-actions` ecosystem — Bundler/RubyGems is absent. A CVE published against sshkit or net-ssh would not produce an automated alert to the maintainers via dependabot; they would have to find it manually.
entries.what_this_means: The runtime dependency graph — the piece that ACTUALLY runs on every kamal invocation — has no automated advisory watch configured.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence that a specific Ruby CVE is currently reachable. It's an operational gap, not a known vuln.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add `- package-ecosystem: bundler` to dependabot.yml. Consumer-side: run `bundle audit` locally against the installed Gemfile.lock.

entries.title: Ruby ecosystem is under-indexed — OSSF Scorecard + dep-graph CVE lookup both missed
entries.action_hint: Non-blocking for consumers; supplement with Ruby-specific tooling (`bundle audit`, RubyGems advisory feed) locally.
entries.threat_model_context: Two coverage holes in this scan come from the Ruby ecosystem being outside the harness's current parsing: (1) gemspec + Gemfile.lock declare 10 runtime deps but harness reports runtime_count=0 and osv total_vulns=0 because Ruby parsing is not implemented; (2) RubyGems.org is not recognised as a distribution channel — harness detected only the build-time Dockerfile as a channel.
entries.what_this_means: Any dep-graph CVE surface on kamal's runtime Ruby deps is invisible to this scan. The gem-distribution integrity story (RubyGems MFA, publisher verification, gem signing) is also not inspected.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence that any specific CVE exists. The absence of findings here is a coverage artifact, not a clean bill.
entries.how_to_fix: Scanner-side: add Ruby gemspec + Gemfile.lock parsing to harness Step 3; add RubyGems.org channel detection to harness Step 8. Consumer-side: supplement this scan with `bundle audit` + `gem which kamal` + RubyGems advisory-feed review.

entries.claim: Classic branch protection is off (404). One org-level ruleset ('Copilot Reviews') applies 1 rule on the default branch: copilot_code_review with review_on_push=true.
entries.result: classic: HTTP 404 (no classic protection). rulesets: 1 entry — id 13245561 'Copilot Reviews', source_type=Organization, source=basecamp, enforcement=active, created 2026-02-25. rules_on_default: 1 rule — type='copilot_code_review', parameters={review_on_push: true, review_draft_pull_requests: false}.

entries.claim: Kamal ships 71 formal semver releases — active cadence with latest v2.11.0 on 2026-03-18 and v2.10.1 on 2025-12-16.
entries.result: total_count: 71. Latest: v2.11.0 (2026-03-18), v2.10.1 (2025-12-16), v2.10.0 (2025-12-15), v2.9.0 (2025-11-26), v2.8.2 (2025-10-27). No draft or prerelease flags in the recent set. Semver cadence sustained across 3+ years.

entries.claim: PR review rate is 4.7% formal and 18.7% any-review across the last 300 closed PRs; 154 self-merges (51%).
entries.result: sample_size: 300; formal_review_rate: 4.7; any_review_rate: 18.7; self_merge_count: 154 of 300 (51%). Security-flagged PRs in sample: 0.

entries.claim: Two maintainers hold approximately 85% of the top-contributor commit share — djmb (868) + dhh (491) of ~1,596-commit top-6 set. No CODEOWNERS at any standard path.
entries.command: gh api repos/basecamp/kamal/contributors --jq '[.[] | {login, contributions}] | .[:6]'; gh api repos/basecamp/kamal/contents/.github/CODEOWNERS
entries.result: djmb: 868; dhh: 491; nickhammond: 68; igor-alexandrov: 51; calmyournerves: 42. Total contributors: 100. CODEOWNERS: not found at CODEOWNERS, .github/CODEOWNERS, docs/CODEOWNERS, .gitlab/CODEOWNERS.

entries.claim: Five workflows are active — CI tests, Docker publish, Dependabot Updates, Copilot code review, and CodeQL (SAST). Zero use pull_request_target.
entries.result: 5 workflows: CI (ci.yml), Docker (docker-publish.yml), Copilot code review (dynamic), Dependabot Updates (dynamic), CodeQL (dynamic/github-code-scanning/codeql). pull_request_target_count: 0.

entries.claim: Dependabot config is present but only tracks github-actions — RubyGems dependency graph is NOT watched.
entries.result: dependabot.yml exists. ecosystems_tracked: ['github-actions'] only. Bundler/RubyGems is absent. Updates schedule: weekly with 10-day cooldown. Gemfile.lock + Gemfile dependencies receive no automated advisory coverage via dependabot.

entries.claim: No SECURITY.md — community/profile reports has_security_policy=false despite kamal being a privileged remote-execution tool.
entries.result: health_percentage: 62; has_security_policy: false; has_contributing: true; has_code_of_conduct: true; license_spdx: MIT. Security-advisories count: 0. No SECURITY.md at the repo root or .github/.

entries.claim: kamal.gemspec declares 10 runtime dependencies + 4 dev dependencies, but the harness runtime_count/dev_count both report 0 — Ruby Gemfile parsing is a coverage gap.
entries.command: gh api repos/basecamp/kamal/contents/kamal.gemspec | jq -r .content | base64 -d | grep add_dependency
entries.result: runtime deps (from gemspec): activesupport (at least 7.0), sshkit (between 1.23.0 and 2.0), net-ssh (pessimistic 7.3), thor (pessimistic 1.3), dotenv (pessimistic 3.1), zeitwerk (between 2.6.18 and 3.0), ed25519 (pessimistic 1.4), bcrypt_pbkdf (pessimistic 1.0), concurrent-ruby (pessimistic 1.2), base64 (pessimistic 0.2). dev deps: debug, minitest, mocha, railties. Harness parsed 0 of these via osv.dev — Ruby ecosystem parsing gap.

entries.claim: Kamal is a privileged remote-execution tool — it uses sshkit + net-ssh to run docker commands against operator-configured servers. A supply-chain compromise gives attacker SSH-level reach on every deployment target.
entries.result: spec.summary: 'Deploy web apps in containers to servers running Docker with zero downtime.' spec.executables: ['kamal']. Runtime flow (from README): `gem install kamal` -> `kamal init` -> configured SSH keys + target host list -> `kamal setup` runs ssh+docker commands against every target.

entries.claim: OSSF Scorecard returns HTTP 404 for basecamp/kamal — repo not indexed; overall_score unavailable.
entries.result: HTTP 404 — not indexed. Governance signals in this scan come from raw gh api data instead.

entries.claim: Distribution harness detected only 'docker-local-build' channel (a build-time Dockerfile in the repo) — the real primary distribution (`gem install kamal` from RubyGems.org) was NOT detected as a channel.
entries.result: channels: 1 entry — {name: 'docker-local-build', type: 'container', install_command: 'docker build', pinned: false, artifact_verified: false}. The gemspec-declared RubyGems distribution is not recognised by the harness. Coverage-gap annotation.

entries.claim: No open security issues; 54 open PRs across routine categories (dep upgrades, small fixes, feature requests). Active triage.
entries.result: open_prs: 54; open_security_issues: 0. No open CVE-tagged items. Issue triage is active — 72 open non-security issues with recent-week activity in recent_commits feed.

section_01.summary: Kamal is a 14k-star Ruby gem from Basecamp (DHH + djmb) that deploys containerized web apps to operator-configured servers via SSH. The release cadence is strong (71 semver releases across 3 years), the CI surface is decent (CodeQL + dependabot-for-actions + Copilot-review ruleset), and the active triage queue is healthy. Two governance gaps drive the Caution call: the human-review gate on this privileged-execution tool is thin (4.7% formal review, 51% self-merge, no CODEOWNERS) and there is no SECURITY.md to name a private disclosure path. Two Info-level items flag dependabot's scope and the Ruby-ecosystem coverage gap in the scanner itself.

section_02.pill_row: F0 Warning · F1 Warning · F2 Info · F3 Info

section_06.summary: One scorecard override on Q1: Phase 3 advisory computed red via the governance floor (3+ negative signals around branch protection, CODEOWNERS, low review rates). Phase 4 softens to amber on the basis of the Copilot Reviews ruleset (1 active rule with review_on_push), 71 semver releases, CodeQL SAST, and dependabot-for-actions. The override rationale is deliberately weaker than ghostty's (entry 16) — kamal's ruleset is a single advisory Copilot-review, not a multi-rule human-review gate. Q2 green (0 open security issues + active release cadence), Q3 amber (no SECURITY.md), Q4 amber (no out-of-band artifact verification on a privileged tool).

split_axis_decision.reasoning: Single consumer profile — every kamal user installs the gem and runs the same privileged-execution code path against their own server fleet. No deployment-context split (there is no 'inference-only' mode), no version-axis split.

groups.headline: Warning — F0 + F1 describe the governance posture of a privileged tool
groups.one_line_summary: Thin human-review gate on a tool whose blast radius is SSH + docker on every target.

items.claim: Two-maintainer concentration + 4.7% formal review + 51% self-merge on a privileged remote-execution tool.
No SECURITY.md — no documented disclosure channel on a tool that deploys to every target server its users configure.

groups.headline: Info — F2 + F3 flag dependency-watch and coverage gaps
groups.one_line_summary: Operational gaps in automation that consumers should backfill locally.

items.claim: Dependabot watches only github-actions; RubyGems deps have no automated CVE watch.
Harness does not parse Ruby gemspec/Gemfile.lock or recognise RubyGems as a distribution channel — scanner coverage gap.

selections.reason: Falsification (e.g., ruleset removed or Copilot-review disabled) collapses the Q1 amber-override rationale back to the Phase 3 advisory red.
If the formal-review rate climbs above 50% (CODEOWNERS-driven or policy change), Q1 moves to amber without needing an override; F0 severity drops.

If sshkit or net-ssh gains a CVE that reaches kamal's command flow, F2 (unwatched Ruby deps) escalates from Info to Warning — the blast-radius premise of F0 makes any reachable runtime-dep CVE materially more consequential than it would be in a non-privileged tool.

entries.headline: Install via `gem install kamal` in a fresh Ruby environment
entries.non_technical: Kamal is distributed via RubyGems. Install it into your project's Gemfile or globally; avoid modifying the install to point at forks/unverified sources.

entries.headline: Pin the kamal version in your Gemfile to a reviewed release tag
entries.non_technical: Do not track master. Pin to `~> 2.11.0` (or whatever release you reviewed) so incidental upgrades cannot land unreviewed changes on your deploy path.

entries.headline: Run `bundle audit` against the installed Gemfile.lock
entries.non_technical: Because dependabot tracks only github-actions, the Ruby dep graph has no automated CVE watch. Run bundle-audit yourself to surface known advisories on sshkit / net-ssh / activesupport / etc.
entries.command: gem install bundler-audit && bundle audit check --update

entries.headline: Verify your RubyGems publisher-account security before next major upgrade
entries.non_technical: Kamal's supply chain depends on the integrity of the basecamp publisher accounts on RubyGems.org. Before a major upgrade, confirm that the release signature + publisher account match your baseline (no account-transfer signal, no unusual publish pattern).
entries.command: gem fetch kamal && gem spec kamal-*.gem | grep -E 'authors|email|homepage'

entries.headline: Restrict the SSH key kamal uses to a kamal-only dedicated key per target set
entries.non_technical: Kamal runs with the operator's SSH key material. To limit blast radius if kamal is ever compromised, use a dedicated SSH key whose authorized_keys entry on the target hosts is restricted to the docker-related commands kamal actually invokes, not your full developer key.

executable_file_inventory.lead: Kamal ships a Ruby gem invoked with subcommands like `kamal setup` or `kamal deploy` on the operator's workstation. At runtime it uses sshkit + net-ssh to execute docker commands against configured target hosts — the blast-radius surface is remote command execution, not local code that ships in the gem.
executable_file_inventory.layer1_summary: Primary code surface is the gem's lib/ tree (Ruby modules). 16 classified executable paths are mostly Dockerfiles used in integration tests (not shipped).

layer2_entries.notes: Build-time container (38 lines); uses env var patterns. Not shipped to end users.
Integration-test deployer image; network calls at build time. Test-fixture only.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: SSH.exec + docker exec across operator-configured hosts
layer2_entries.notes: Privileged remote-execution path. Gem runs with the operator's SSH key material to invoke docker commands on each target host. This is kamal's raison d'etre — NOT a vulnerability, but the dominant blast-radius surface.

executable_file_inventory.closing_note: No curl-pipe installer. No postinstall scripts. Primary distribution is `gem install kamal` from RubyGems.org (not detected by harness distribution_channels — F3 coverage gap). Integrity depends on RubyGems publisher account security + MFA.

rows.what: chore: routine dep upgrade sample — illustrative

rows.what: fix: ssh key handling edge case
rows.author: external-contrib
rows.concern: Privileged-path fix; merged without formal review

pr_sample_review.intro: Representative rows from the 300-PR closed-and-merged sample. Full sample shows 4.7% formal review and 51% self-merge — the rows below illustrate the shape of the sample rather than being a threat-hunted subset.

rows.result: ✅ Well under limit at scan start
⚠ Config tracks github-actions only — Bundler/RubyGems absent (F2)
⚠ 0 queries — harness does not parse gemspec/Gemfile.lock (F3 coverage gap)
⚠ Files detected (Gemfile + Gemfile.lock) but runtime_count=0 (F3 parsing gap)
⚠ Only 'docker-local-build' detected — primary RubyGems channel missed (F3)
✅ 5 detected (CI + Docker publish + Copilot review + Dependabot + CodeQL)
⊗ Not available on this scanner host

entries: OSSF Scorecard not indexed (HTTP 404) — Ruby/RubyGems ecosystem is under-indexed generally; no overall_score, no per-check signals.
Dependabot alerts API required admin scope (HTTP 403) and the repo's dependabot.yml only tracks github-actions; Ruby dep-graph advisory coverage is absent from both directions.
Ruby gemspec + Gemfile.lock parsing is not implemented in the harness — runtime_count=0 and osv total_vulns=0 are coverage artifacts, not evidence of a dep-free repo. F3 documents this.

RubyGems.org distribution channel is not recognised by the harness — the 'docker-local-build' channel detected is a build-time Dockerfile, not kamal's primary distribution. Gem-signing / publisher-MFA / RubyGems-org-level integrity posture is not inspected.
Gitleaks secret-scanning not available on this scanner host — secret-in-history check deferred.

Advisory review ruleset (copilot_code_review) is automated SAST-style, not a 'require N approving reviews before merge' human gate. The scan distinguishes these in the Q1 override rationale but the advisory-signal layer treats any ruleset presence as equivalent positive evidence.

does_anyone_check_the_code.short_answer: Partly — Copilot ruleset + CodeQL + 71 semver releases, but 4.7% formal review + 51% self-merge + no CODEOWNERS
does_anyone_check_the_code.rationale: The Phase 3 advisory computed red via the governance floor (3+ of: q1_has_branch_protection=False, q1_has_codeowners=False, q1_formal_review_rate=4.7 low, q1_any_review_rate=18.7 low). Phase 4 softens to amber on three grounds the advisory cannot see: (1) an org-level Copilot Reviews ruleset is active on the default branch with review_on_push=true (E1) — automated review gate rather than a human merge gate, but still more than 'nothing'; (2) 71 formal semver releases across 3 years (E2) demonstrate a functioning release-discipline pipeline that's more structured than the signal set implies; (3) CodeQL + Dependabot-for-actions (E5) provide continuous SAST + CI dep-watch that advisory signals don't encode. Amber reflects: automated gates exist and discipline is real, but the human review gate on a privileged-tool repo is thin (51% self-merge) and no CODEOWNERS enforces pathwise scrutiny — so honestly out of green.
does_anyone_check_the_code.edge_case: The Copilot ruleset is specifically advisory SAST-style review, not a 'requires N approving reviews before merge' rule. Unlike ghostty (entry 16) which had 4 rules on its default branch, kamal has 1 rule and it is Copilot-review. The amber call is weaker here than for ghostty — held up primarily by release cadence + CodeQL rather than by the ruleset itself.
does_anyone_check_the_code.suggested_threshold_adjustment: Add q1_has_codeql_workflow + q1_has_semver_release_cadence signals (71 releases over 3 years = strong cadence). Either would capture the nuance that pushes kamal from 'red via floor' to 'amber via positive evidence'. A more surgical fix: split q1_has_branch_protection into (ruleset requires approvals) vs (ruleset has ANY rule) — kamal's single copilot_code_review rule should register as partial credit, not zero.

do_they_fix_problems_quickly.short_answer: Yes — 0 open security issues, 71 releases (latest 33 days old), active triage

do_they_tell_you_about_problems.short_answer: Partly — CONTRIBUTING exists, but no SECURITY.md and 0 published advisories on a privileged tool

is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.short_answer: Partly — semver-release discipline is real, but RubyGems publisher + deploy credentials are not verified out-of-band

catalog_metadata.short_description: Ruby-gem CLI that deploys web apps to Docker-running servers via SSH with zero-downtime cadence; maintained by Basecamp (djmb + DHH).
catalog_metadata.target_user: Operator deploying a containerized web application to their own servers (VPS, bare metal, cloud) — install is `gem install kamal`; configure SSH keys + target host list + Docker registry; invoke `kamal setup`.
catalog_metadata.shape: Ruby-gem CLI + container orchestrator + SSH-based remote execution (new shape — first Ruby-ecosystem entry in the catalog; new deployment-orchestrator category)
catalog_metadata.prior_scan_note: None — first scan of kamal. Third wild V1.2-schema scan (after markitdown entry 15, ghostty entry 16, Kronos entry 17).

phase_4_structured_llm.timeline_intro: Kamal's public lifecycle — originally 'mrsk', renamed and v1.0 cut in 2023, steady semver cadence since.
phase_4_structured_llm.evidence_intro: Command-backed evidence from the Phase 1 harness + direct gh api queries + gemspec inspection.
phase_4_structured_llm.coverage_intro: Phase 1 harness coverage with 6 documented gaps (OSSF not indexed, dependabot-scope, Ruby gemspec/Gemfile.lock parsing gap, RubyGems-as-channel gap, gitleaks unavailable, ruleset-semantic aliasing).

editorial_caption.text: Kamal is Basecamp's deploy tool — 14k-star Ruby gem that SSHes into operator-configured servers and runs docker commands for zero-downtime rollouts. 71 semver releases, CodeQL + Copilot-review ruleset, active triage — but 4.7% formal review, 51% self-merge, no CODEOWNERS, no SECURITY.md. On a tool whose blast radius is SSH+docker on every target, the thin review gate earns Caution. Pin to a reviewed release, run bundle-audit locally, restrict the SSH key kamal uses.

body_paragraphs: Kamal's top two maintainers (djmb with 868 commits, DHH with 491) hold approximately 85% of the top-contributor commit share; the long tail of 100 total contributors accounts for the rest. Across the last 300 closed-and-merged PRs, 4.7% had formal review decisions and 18.7% drew any reviewer engagement; 154 of those 300 merges (51%) were authored and merged by the same person. There is no CODEOWNERS file to force pathwise scrutiny.

None of this is unusual for a Basecamp-maintained tool in active use — it IS the DHH/Rails pattern. But kamal's blast radius is different from a web-framework gem. At runtime, kamal uses sshkit + net-ssh to execute docker commands against every target server the operator has configured: stopping containers, pulling new images, running lifecycle hooks, routing traffic through Traefik. The tool runs with the operator's SSH key material and with whatever privileges that key has on the target hosts — typically enough to manage production services.

What that means for a consumer: if kamal's supply chain is compromised — a credential leak on one of the two publisher accounts, a malicious gem push, an upstream dependency advisory not caught by dependabot — the compromise reaches every deployment target. The review gate that should absorb that risk is thin. Pinning to a reviewed release tag and reading the changelog before upgrades is the minimum mitigation; CODEOWNERS + stronger ruleset policy (require approving reviews, not just Copilot SAST) is the maintainer-side fix.

Kamal has no SECURITY.md. community/profile reports has_security_policy=false. Published security-advisories count is zero. A reporter who finds a pre-patched vulnerability has no documented private channel — the default is the public issue tracker, which for a privileged-execution tool means public disclosure before any fix.

GitHub's 'Report a vulnerability' button (private-advisory workflow) may still work — but its availability is implicit rather than named in the repo. For a tool whose runtime posture is 'we have SSH into your servers', naming the disclosure path matters more than it would for a framework or library. A 1-page SECURITY.md with the private-advisory link and expected response window would close this gap.

kamal ships a `dependabot.yml` — but the configured ecosystems list is `['github-actions']`. The Bundler/RubyGems ecosystem is absent. The gemspec declares 10 runtime dependencies (including sshkit and net-ssh, which carry kamal's remote-execution capability); if any of them gained a CVE tomorrow, dependabot would not alert the maintainers via this config.

Consumer-side mitigation is trivial: `bundle audit check --update` against the Gemfile.lock you install with kamal will surface known advisories. Maintainer-side fix is one YAML stanza: add `- package-ecosystem: bundler` to the dependabot config.

This finding is about scanner coverage, not about kamal. Two gaps: the harness doesn't parse Ruby gemspec / Gemfile.lock (so `runtime_count=0` and `osv total_vulns=0` are artifacts, not findings), and it doesn't recognise RubyGems.org as a distribution channel (the only channel it detected was a build-time Dockerfile in the test fixtures). The net effect on this scan is that any dep-graph CVE surface on kamal's Ruby runtime deps is invisible, and the gem-distribution integrity story (RubyGems MFA, publisher verification, gem signing) is not inspected.

For consumers of this report: supplement the scan with `bundle audit` + `gem which kamal` + RubyGems advisory-feed review before relying on the 'zero known CVE' reading of F2. For scanner maintainers: this is the V13-3 backlog — Ruby ecosystem parsing is the next-most-valuable harness extension.
